coredata.dc:title: N-functionalized graphene quantum dots: Charge transporting layer for high-rate and durable Li4Ti5O12-based Li-ion battery
coredata.dc:creator: Khan, Firoz
Oh, Misol
Kim, Jae Hyun
coredata.dc:description: Spinel Li4Ti5O12 can replace carbon in Li-ion battery anodes due to its high voltage, preventing decomposition of the electrolyte and formation of Li metal dendrites. However, Li4Ti5O12 has a low electronic conductivity and Li-ion diffusion coefficient, limiting its charge/discharge properties at high rate capacities, and also suffers from gassing during cycling. Here, we used N-functionalized graphene quantum dots interfacial layer, which (1) protects Li4Ti5O12 from ambient degradation, (2) forms a thin and smooth solid-electrolyte interphase layer on the Li4Ti5O12 surface, (3) acts as a charge transfer layer, (4) protects the Li4Ti5O12 electrode from reactions with the electrolyte, and (5) suppresses gassing during cycling. Consequently, the Li-ion diffusion coefficient increased by ∼19%. The effectiveness of the N-functionalized graphene quantum dots is manifested in the specific capacity of 161 mAh/g at 50C, which is improved by ∼23% compared to pure Li4Ti5O12 electrode and maintained for over 500 cycles. Unlike graphene, N-functionalized graphene quantum dots themselves work as a stable charge transporting and protecting layer. Our strategy successfully obtained a good cycling performance and long cycling life of Li4Ti5O12 at high C-rates.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dots
LTO anode
Li-ion battery
N-functionalization
Durable electrode
Gassing behavior

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure e-component body acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast footnotes grantnumber grantsponsor highlightsabst orcid primabst ref specialabst

xocs:hub-sec.xocs:hub-sec-title: Novel Materials for Energy and Advanced Applications

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Bonding and elemental characterization of the composite samples. (a) FTIR spectra, (b) XPS survey spectra, (c) high-resolution XPS spectra of Ti 2p, and (d) high-resolution XPS spectra of O 1 s of LTO and LTO-NGQ.

Comparison of pristine LTO and LTO-NGQ20 electrodes. (a) Rate capability from 0.2C to 50C, (b) long cycle performance at 20C for 200 cycles, (c) repeated rate capability from 0.2C to 50C, and (d) long cycle performance at 50C. The capacities of the LTO-NGQ20 electrode at 20C and 50C were 97% and 91% of the capacity at 1C, respectively.

Charge transfer enhancement via N-GQD-encapsulation. (a) b-value determination of the peak cathodic current density for the scan rates of 0.1 to 1.0 mV/s and (b) Warburg coefficient determination from the low-frequency region of EIS curves for pristine LTO and LTO-NGQ20 electrodes. (c, d) Schematic diagrams showing the formation of the hierarchical LTO/N-GQD/Super-P structure.
Microstructural characterization of the composite electrodes after cycling for 500 cycles at various C-rates. TEM images of (a, b) pristine LTO and (c, d) LTO-NGQ20 electrodes.

Gassing mechanism in LTO electrode and its suppression via N-GQD-encapsulation. Schematic diagrams of the phase change and SEI formation in (a) pristine LTO and (b) LTO-NGQ20 electrodes during cycling. Schematic diagrams of the gassing behavior in (a) pristine LTO and (b) LTO-NGQ20 electrodes during cycling.

Elemental analysis of the composite electrodes after cycling for 500 cycles at various C-rates. High-resolution XPS spectra of (a) C 1 s for cycled, (b) O 1 s for cycled, (c) Ti 2p for as-prepared and cycled, (d) F 1 s for as-prepared and cycled, (e) Li 1 s for as-prepared and cycled, and (f) P 2p for as-prepared and cycled electrodes of LTO and LTO-NGQ.

head.ce:title: N-functionalized graphene quantum dots: Charge transporting layer for high-rate and durable Li4Ti5O12-based Li-ion battery

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Center of Research Excellence in Renewable Energy (CoRERE), King Fahd University of Petroleum and Minerals (KFUPM), Dhahran 31261, Saudi Arabia

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Center of Research Excellence in Renewable Energy (CoRERE)
King Fahd University of Petroleum and Minerals (KFUPM)

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Smart Textile Convergence Research Group, Daegu Gyeongbuk Institute of Science & Technology, 333, Techno Jungang-Daero, Hyeonpung-Myeon, Dalseong-Gun, Daegu 42988, Republic of Korea

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Daegu Gyeongbuk Institute of Science & Technology

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Spinel Li4Ti5O12 can replace carbon in Li-ion battery anodes due to its high voltage, preventing decomposition of the electrolyte and formation of Li metal dendrites. However, Li4Ti5O12 has a low electronic conductivity and Li-ion diffusion coefficient, limiting its charge/discharge properties at high rate capacities, and also suffers from gassing during cycling. Here, we used N-functionalized graphene quantum dots interfacial layer, which (1) protects Li4Ti5O12 from ambient degradation, (2) forms a thin and smooth solid-electrolyte interphase layer on the Li4Ti5O12 surface, (3) acts as a charge transfer layer, (4) protects the Li4Ti5O12 electrode from reactions with the electrolyte, and (5) suppresses gassing during cycling. Consequently, the Li-ion diffusion coefficient increased by ∼19%. The effectiveness of the N-functionalized graphene quantum dots is manifested in the specific capacity of 161 mAh/g at 50C, which is improved by ∼23% compared to pure Li4Ti5O12 electrode and maintained for over 500 cycles. Unlike graphene, N-functionalized graphene quantum dots themselves work as a stable charge transporting and protecting layer. Our strategy successfully obtained a good cycling performance and long cycling life of Li4Ti5O12 at high C-rates.

ce:section.ce:para: As modern transportation depends heavily on fossil fuels [1], one of the most effective ways to reduce CO2 emissions and cope with the increasing scarcity of fossil fuels is to use electrical vehicles (EVs) or hybrid electrical vehicles (HEVs) [2]. Recently, considerable efforts have been devoted to the development of high-performance Li-ion batteries (LIBs) for new applications including plug-in HEVs and EVs [3–7]. Although LIBs have the advantage of intrinsically high energy density [8], the current LIB technologies have short cycling life, low power density, and safety hazards and therefore, cannot satisfy the requirements of efficient storage of renewable energy and/or powering EVs or HEVs [9]. Therefore, the development of electrode materials with high energy and power densities, and a long lifespan is urgently required [10].

Lithium titanate (Li4Ti5O12; LTO) has received considerable attention as a LIB anode material owing to its outstanding high-rate capacity and cycling stability, and its improved safety compared to graphite [11,12]. This material has a high and flat Li insertion/extraction voltage (∼1.55 V vs. Li/Li+) and good structural stability with negligible volume change during Li insertion/extraction; this prevents the growth of Li metal dendrites and the reduction decomposition of the electrolyte and provides long cycle life and high-rate capability [13]. Unfortunately, the high-rate performance of LTO cannot be achieved due to its low intrinsic electronic conductivity [14] and moderate lithium-ion diffusion coefficient [15]. Accordingly, various strategies have demonstrated significant improvement in the rate capability of LTO anodes, including the incorporation of conductive phases such as metal or carbonaceous materials [16,17], control of the morphology and microstructure [18–20], and ion doping [21].

The large-scale application of LTO-based LIBs suffers from serious gassing and associated swelling during charge/discharge cycles, making storage challenging [22]. This is generally caused by electrolyte decomposition owing to interfacial reactions [23,24]. Surface coating with a conductive carbon layer is widely used to enhance electronic and ionic transport within LTO, significantly improving the electrochemical performance [25,26]. Such a barrier layer can effectively suppress gassing due to interfacial reactions [27,28] because, during the cycling process, a thicker layer of solid-electrolyte interface (SEI) is formed on carbon-coated electrode [24,28], which protect from the surface reaction of LTO with electrolyte [22]. Both the formation and decomposition of SEI layer causes gassing. With the increase in temperature, the SEI layer becomes unstable and started to decompose its carbonate species, which is accountable for gassing [22]. Also, the carbon materials have high reactivity with electrolyte solutions at elevated temperatures, which raises safety concerns [29]. Therefore, alternative surface coating layers of inorganic materials have been proposed, such as ZnO [30], AlF3 [31], NiOx [32], and TiNx [33], that has negligible reactivity with the electrolyte. Surface modification of LTO resulted in a smoother and thinner SEI layer [30]. However, LTO electrode coated with these inorganic materials has the poor capacity [30–33].

Here, we propose a facile method for coating LTO with an ultrathin protective and charge-transport layer of N-functionalized graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs) via a solution-based process (LTO-NGQ). The electrochemical performances of LTO-NGQ, including cycling stability (200 cycles at 20C followed by 100 cycles at 50C) and rate capability (0.2–50C), were extensively evaluated in half cells.

Graphite flakes of approximately 325 mesh (99.995% purity) were purchased from Alfa Aesar. Sodium nitrate (NaNO3; > 99%) and polyethyleneimine (PEI-2000; 50% wt. in water) were purchased from Aldrich. Sulphuric acid (H2SO4, 98%) and potassium permanganate (KMnO4; 99.6%) were purchased from Fisher Scientific.

Prior to oxidation, 2 g of graphite flakes were purified by adding them to 30 mL of hydrofluoric acid (HF). The solution was mechanical stirred to obtain a homogeneous mixture, which was washed several times with deionized (DI) water, then acetone, and finally dried in a vacuum oven at 40 °C. The modified Hummers’ method was used to prepare graphite oxide (GO) from the purified graphite flakes. First, 2 g of graphite powder was mixed with 1 g of NaNO3 in a glass beaker. The mixture was slowly added to 96 mL of concentrated H2SO4 in a glass beaker placed in an ice bath. After vigorous stirring, 6 g of KMnO4 was gradually added to the mixture. Initially, the temperature was maintained at approximately 20 °C for 1 h. Then, the mixture was transferred to a water bath and stirred for 21 h at 35 °C to obtain a brown paste. DI water (150 mL) was slowly added to the paste while the temperature was maintained below 50 °C. The mixture was diluted with 240 mL of DI water, and 5 mL of H2O2 was added to oxidize the remaining graphite. The resultant solution was filtered and washed with dilute (approximately 5%) HCl to remove metal ions, and then washed with DI water to increase the pH value. Finally, the GO was washed with anhydrous ethanol and freeze-dried to obtain GO powder.

The resulting GO powder was dissolved in DI water to obtain a concentration of 10.0 mg/mL and the mixture was sonicated for 2 h. Subsequently, 5 mL of the GO solution was added to 40 mL of 16 mol/mL HNO3 solution and refluxed at 120 °C for 24 h. Subsequently, a 10 wt% aqueous solution of NaOH was added to adjust the pH value to 7. Finally, 0.1 mL of PEI was added to the GO solution. Then, the mixture was stirred at 85 °C for 1 h using a magnetic stirrer to produce functionalized GO; this solution was transferred to a steel-lined Teflon autoclave and hydrothermally treated at 200 °C for 12 h, producing an aqueous solution of N-GQDs. However, undoped GQDs were synthesized via same process without using PEI.

First, Li2CO3 (2.536 g) and anatase TiO2 (6.657 g) were added to 200 mL of acetone and the mixture was ball milled for 24 h. After drying the mixture, it was annealed at 750 °C for 1 h (with a heating rate of 5 °C /min) to obtain LTO powder. For the surface modification of LTO, an aqueous solution of N-GQDs and LTO powder was placed in a beaker and the pH of the solution was adjusted to 2 by adding H3PO4. Finally, the mixture was filtered and dried at 70 °C to obtain surface-modified LTO with N-GQDs. Henceforth, the modified LTO with N-GQDs is referred to as LTO-NGQ. We evaluated the effect of the N-GQDs concentration by adding either 10, 20, or 30 mL of the aqueous N-GQDs solution.

The elemental compositions of the LTO and LTO-NGQ powder were measured using XPS in an ultrahigh vacuum setup equipped with a monochromatic Al Kα X-ray source (1486.6 eV) (Escalab 250Xi, Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA). The binding energies were calibrated using the graphite C 1 s peak. Gaussian–Lorentzian combined peaks were used to fit the XPS data with background correction using the Shirley method. High-resolution TEM (HR-TEM; HF-3300/NB5000/S-4800, Hitachi, Japan) was employed for examining the atomic structure of the sample. Nitrogen adsorption–desorption experiments were performed at 77 K on a (Micromeritics, Flex 3.02, USA) and Barrett-Joyner-Halenda (BJH) pore size distributions were derived from the desorption isotherms. The specific surface area was calculated using the Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) method. Thermogravimetric measurements were performed in the temperature range of 25–600 °C in air (flow rate of 40 mL/min) using a thermogravimetric analyzer (TGA; STD Q600, TA Instruments-Waters Korea). Fourier-transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy (Continuum, Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA) was performed in attenuated total reflectance mode at a resolution of 4 cm−1.

A slurry was prepared by ball milling a mixture containing 80 wt% anode material, 12 wt% Super P, and 8 wt% polyvinylidene fluoride (PVDF) dissolved in n-methyl pyrrolidone solvent. The paste was coated on an aluminum foil using the doctor-blade method. The working electrode was dried at 110 °C in a vacuum oven for 12 h and then pressed to a final thickness of 40–60 μm. Finally, slurry-coated Al sheet electrodes with a diameter of 14 mm were cut (cell area = 1.5386 cm2). The loading level of active materials was ∼1 mg/cm2. Electrochemical cells were assembled in an Ar-filled glove box with metallic lithium foil as the counter and reference electrodes, a polypropylene membrane as the separator, and 1 M LiPF6 in a mixture of ethyl carbonate and dimethyl carbonate (3:7 v/v) as the electrolyte. The charge/discharge cycles were measured using a battery tester (WMPG1000, WonATech) over a potential range of 1.0–3.0 V vs. Li/Li+ at different C-rates (1C = 175 mAh/g). EIS measurements were carried out using a two-electrode system with a potentiostat (VersaSTAT3, Princeton Applied Research, USA) with Li as the counter and reference electrodes and the LTO/ LTO-NGQ sample as the working electrode. The EIS test was performed by applying an AC voltage of amplitude 10 mV over the frequency range of 100 kHz to 5 mHz at a constant voltage of 1.6 V (vs. Li/Li+).

The FTIR absorption spectra obtained to investigate the bond signatures of LTO and LTO-NGQ are shown in Fig. 1 a. In the pristine LTO, two intense absorption peaks were observed at 1445 and 1508 cm−1, corresponding to the asymmetric C-O stretching in Li2CO3 [34]. However, such bond signatures were not clearly observed in the LTO-NGQ samples. The appearance of the bond signature of C-O asymmetric stretching in the LTO sample was due to the exposure of LTO to air, whereas in the LTO-NGQ samples, the N-GQDs protected the LTO surface from direct contact with air. The presence of H2O in the air could result in a proton-assisted H+/Li+ exchange reaction occurring at the surface, forming Li2CO3. The Li-truncated surface of Li4Ti5O12 readily adsorbed H2O and produced LiOH and Li4-xHTi5O12. Subsequently, LiOH reacted with CO2 and formed Li2CO3, reducing the stability of LTO [34]. A weak broad absorption peak was observed at 1366 cm−1, attributed to the C-O symmetric stretching mode. Two strong broad absorption bands observed at 3000–3500 cm−1 were assigned to the stretching vibrations of O–H (centered at 3129 cm−1) and N–H (centered at 3427 cm−1) in N-GQDs, which were absent in the spectra of the pristine LTO sample [35]. A broad and weak absorption band observed at 1269–1493 cm−1 (centred at 1366 cm−1) was assigned to the C-O symmetric vibration in the LTO-NGQ30 sample due to the large amount of N-GQDs on the surface of LTO. Moreover, the absorption bands at 400–660 cm−1, assigned to the Li-O and Ti-O vibrations of LTO, were observed for all samples (Fig. S1 of Supplementary Information) [36,37]. Additionally, two bond signatures at 935 and 970 cm−1 attributed to C–C and C–N bonding, respectively, were observed in the LTO-NGQ samples due to the presence of N-GQDs.

XPS analysis was used to determine the elemental composition and study the Ti valence state. The XPS survey scans of the LTO and LTO-NGQ samples are shown in Fig. 1b. Peaks corresponding to Li, Ti, O, and C were observed in the LTO sample, while additional N 1 s and P 2p peaks were observed for all LTO-NGQ samples. The N peak was attributed to N-GQDs, while the P peak was attributed to traces of phosphoric acid used during processing. Fig. 1c shows a high-resolution scan of the Ti 2p peaks of pristine LTO and LTO-NGQ samples. The deconvoluted peaks of Ti 2p3/2 and Ti 2p1/2 are shown in Fig. S2(a)–(d) of Supplementary Information. The main peaks of LTO assigned to a Ti4+ state (Ti 2p3/2 at 457.6 eV and Ti 2p1/2 at 463.5 eV) were slightly shifted toward the lower energy Ti3+ state after the introduction of N-GQDs [38], due to a slight reduction in the Ti valence state of the outermost LTO surface, which has been shown to enhance the electronic conductivity and discharge capacity [39,40]. In the case of LTO-NGQ30, three additional peaks (at 458.4 eV, 459.9 eV, and 464.7 eV) of TiO2 were observed (Fig. S2(d) of Supporting Information). The positions of the Ti3+ and Ti4+ convoluted peaks corresponding to Ti 2p3/2 are listed in Table S1 of Supplementary Information. The Ti3+ fraction was determined to be 2.56%, 22.92%, 31.76%, and 117.92% for LTO, LTO-NGQ10, LTO-NGQ20, and LTO-NGQ30, respectively. Hence, reduction of the Ti valence state observed here occurred via a different mechanism than that of other Ti-based materials, where a higher number of Ti3+ defects were obtained in an inert or reducing atmosphere [41,42]; our method can significantly simplify the heat treatment and reduce production costs of such materials. Fig. 1d shows a detailed O 1 s XPS scan, confirming the reduction of the Ti valence state at the outermost surface of LTO. The peak at 529.9 eV (Ti-O) also shifted toward lower binding energy due to the reduction of oxides. The peak position finally shifted to 528.5 eV for LTO-NGQ30. These shifts were attributed to increased electron density around O atoms in LTO due to the N-GQDs layer.

Moreover, the C 1 s spectra of LTO-NGQ samples were deconvoluted into four main peaks (Fig. S3(a)–(c) of Supplementary Information) to determine the fraction of C-bonding. The C 1 s peak was deconvoluted into four parts corresponding to C–C/CC (∼284.6 eV), C–O/CN (∼285.6 eV, hydroxyl and epoxy), CO/C–N (∼286.9 eV, carbonyl), and O–CO (∼288.3 eV, carboxyl) [43]. The peak intensities corresponding to C–C/CC and C–O/CN decreased with increasing N-GQDs volume, while the intensities of the other two peaks increased accordingly. The bond percentages are shown in Supplementary Fig. S3d. The O–CO bond percentages were 6.43%, 12.38%, and 29.74% for N-GQDs volumes of 10, 20, and 30 mL, respectively, indicating that the quality of N-GQDs on the LTO surface degraded with increasing N-GQDs content; hence, the conductivity of LTO-NGQ-30 is expected to be the lowest of all samples.

Field-emission SEM images of LTO and LTO-NGQ20 are shown in Fig. S4(a) and S4(b) of Supplementary Information, respectively. The morphology of the particles in both samples was similar, with particle sizes in the range of 50–200 nm. The corresponding specific surface areas were 7.54 and 7.67 m3/g for LTO and LTO-NGQ20, respectively (Fig. S5(a) of Supplementary Information). Moreover, the pore diameters of LTO and LTO-NGQ20 were 46.1 and 47.5 nm, respectively (Fig. S5(b) of Supplementary Information). After the introduction of N-GQDs, the increase in the specific surface area of LTO was only ∼1.7%, which was not expected to significantly affect the specific capacity. HRTEM images of LTO and LTO-NGQ20 are shown in Fig. S6(a,b) and S6(c,d) of Supplementary Information, respectively. In the LTO-NGQ20 sample, a ∼ 3 nm N-GQDs layer was deposited. The TEM image of N-GQDs (Fig. S6(e) of Supplementary Information) shows N-GQD particles of ∼ 6 nm in diameter.

TGA was performed to determine the ratio of GQDs in LTO-NGQ20 (Fig. S6(f) of Supplementary Information). After heating the LTO and LTO-NGQ20 samples to 600 °C, weight losses of 1.04% and 2.38%, respectively, were observed. The weight ratio of N-GQDs/LTO in LTO-NGQ20 was approximately 1.34%. Most of the weight loss in the LTO sample was due to the removal of oxygen-containing compounds from the LTO surface. Four sharp peaks (marked 1–4) were observed for LTO-NGQ20; the peaks labeled 1 and 2 were due to the removal of the oxygen-bearing functional groups, while peaks 3 and 4 were due to the removal of N-bearing function groups [44,45]. Coloured EDS maps for N-GQDs and LTO-NGQ20 (Figs. S7 and S8 of Supplementary Information, respectively) were obtained to show the elemental distribution. A minute quantity of P was found in LTO-NGQ20 due to traces of H3PO4, which is disappeared after electrode formation.

The anode active materials were coated on an Al foil for electrochemical characterization. SEM images of the electrochemical samples using LTO and LTO-NGQ20 are shown in Fig. S9(a) and S9(b) of Supplementary Information, respectively, where similar morphologies were observed in both cases. However, in the LTO-NGQ20 electrode, the density of the Super P conductive material attached to the LTO was higher than that of the electrode prepared with pristine LTO.

Fig. 2 a compares the rate capability of the LTO and LTO-NGQ20 electrodes at various C-rates (0.2C, 0.5C, 1C, 5C, 10C, 20C, and 50C) over the voltage range of 1.0–3.0 V vs. Li/Li+. The measured capacities of LTO at 1C, 20C, and 50C were 160, 155, and 131 mAh/g, respectively. However, the specific capacities of LTO-NGQ20 were higher; 177, 167, and 155 mAh/g at 1C, 20C, and 50C, respectively. In addition, the discharge capacity was slightly higher with the increasing number of cycles. Lower capacities were obtained for LTO-NGQ10 and LTO-NGQ30, compared to LTO-NGQ20 (Fig. S10(a) of Supplementary Information). A slight improvement in the capacity is obtained for undoped GQDs coated LTO electrodes (results are not included). The undoped GQDs are similar to carbonaceous materials. However, N-GQDs are a composition of graphene quantum dots and N-related functional groups. N provide the lone pair electrons to enhance the charge transfer rate that results for enhancement in the capacity of LTO-NGQ electrode. Therefore, the insertion of N-GQD layer promotes the lithiation/de-lithiation process. The lower capacity of LTO-NGQ10 may be due to insufficient thickness of the N-GQDs layer on the LTO surface. In LTO-NGQ10, the fraction of Ti3+ increased to 22.92% compared with that of pristine LTO (2.56%) (Table S1 of Supplementary Information).

In addition, the long cycle stability was studied at 20C for 200 cycles (Fig. 2b). The discharge-specific capacities of LTO and LTO-NGQ20 electrodes were enhanced to 157 and 171 mAh/g, respectively. The capacity was stable over the entire range. The cyclic capacities of both samples were further measured at similar C-rates (Fig. 2c). The cyclic capacities increased slightly and exhibited better stability than the initial results. The capacities of LTO-NGQ20 at 1C, 20C, and 50C were determined to be 177, 171, and 157 mAh/g, respectively. The values for pure LTO were 159 mAh/g at 1C, 153 mAh/g at 20C, and 130 mAh/g at 50C. Moreover, long cycle stability was tested at 50C for 100 cycles (Fig. 2d) and at 100C for 30 cycles (Fig. S10(b) of Supplementary Information). The specific capacity of LTO-NGQ20 at 50C increased from 157 mAh/g to 159 mAh/g after six cycles, further increased to 161 mAh/g after 15 cycles, and remained constant without evident decay for over 100 cycles. However, the capacity of LTO at 50C fluctuated between 125 and 131 mAh/g. Such capacity fluctuation occurred due to insertion/extraction of Li+ in individual LTO grains is inhomogeneous in different cycles, which results for nonuniform electrochemical reaction. Therefore, the capacity has fluctuated. A similar observation was made in aggregated LTO electrode by other groups [46,47].

The specific capacity of LTO-NGQ20 at 100C varied between 109 and 113 mAh/g up to 13 cycles, then remained constant at 117 mAh/g thereafter (for LTO it varied between 11 and 18 mAh/g). The specific capacities obtained at high C-rates in the present work are compared with previously reported values for LTO-based anodes (Table S2 of Supplementary Information).

The charge–discharge profiles of LTO and LTO-NGQ20 at 5C are shown in Fig. S10(c) of Supplementary Information. Both electrodes exhibited voltage plateaus at ∼ 1.55 V owing to the two-phase redox reaction based on the Ti3+/Ti4+ redox couple [48], which could prevent reductive decomposition of the electrolyte and formation of lithium metal dendrites [49]. The LTO-NGQ20 electrode exhibited a flatter plateau and higher capacity (due to the reduction of the Ti valence state) [50] than the pristine LTO. The polarisation between the charge and discharge plateaus of LTO and LTO-NGQ20 was 65 mV and 53 mV, respectively (inset of Fig. S10(c)), demonstrating improved kinetics of LTO after the introduction of N-GQDs. This improvement in the electrochemical properties was due to the presence of Ti3+ (as observed during XPS analysis). The two main factors influencing the electrochemical performance of LTO-NGQ were the reduction of the Ti valence state from Ti4+ to Ti3+ and improved charge transfer due to the N-GQDs hieratical layer (between LTO and the conducting material); therefore, the N-GQDs concentration controls the electrode performance. During the reduction of the outer surface of LTO, the O content in N-GQDs hieratical layer is increased which reduced the conductivity of N-GQDs layer. Thus, the electrochemical performance increased up to the addition of 20 mL of N-GQDs; with the further addition of 30 mL of N-GQDs the performance slightly decreased due to poorer charge transfer due to the N-GQDs hierarchical layer.

CV studies were carried out to further investigate the effect of N-GQDs on the Li storage capacity of the LTO electrode at various scan rates (not shown here). The specific capacity enhancement observed at the scan rates of 0.1, 0.2, 0.5, and 1 mV/s was 10.75%, 10.94%, 10.99%, and 11.72% for N-GQD-encapsulated samples. CV curves at a scan rate of 1 mV/s of LTO and LTO-NGQ20 were compared to study the degree of polarisation (Fig. S10(d) of Supplementary Information). Anodic (Li de-intercalation) and cathodic (Li intercalation) peaks of LTO electrode were observed at 1.767 V and 1.416 V, respectively, which shifted to 1.734 V and 1.464 V, respectively, for the LTO-NGQ20 electrode. The spacing between the anodic and cathodic peaks of LTO-NGQ20 (0.270 V) was smaller than that of the LTO electrode (0.351 V); hence, electrode polarisation was reduced for the LTO-NGQ20 electrode [51]. The plots of log (jp) vs. log (v) for the LTO and LTO-NGQ20 electrodes (Fig. 3 a) were used to determine the b-value of the electrodes using the power-law relationship with the scan rate (jp = avb ) [13,52]. Where a and b are adjustable parameters, jp is the cathodic peak current density, v is the scan rate, and the b-value provides insight into the charge storage mechanism. The b-value obtained for LTO and LTO-NGQ20 was 0.591 and 0.692, respectively. The b-value of LTO indicates that the kinetics were mainly diffusion controlled, while it indicated the excellent high-rate capacity for LTO-NGQ20 (and was slightly higher than that of a triple-phase LTO/TiO2(anatase)/TiO2(rutile) electrode) [13].

The Warburg coefficient (σ) was determined from the slope of the straight line in a Zre vs. ω-1/2 plot (Fig. 3b), where ω is the angular frequency, and then used to determine the diffusion coefficient of the Li-ions (DLi), which is inversely proportional to the square of σ. The corresponding slopes of the LTO and LTO-NGQ20 electrodes were 45.43 and 41.63, respectively, indicating that the diffusion coefficient increased by approximately 19% with N-GQDs coating. A schematic showing the N-GQDs coated onto the LTO surface and the mechanism for enhancing charge diffusion and surface conductivity of LTO are shown in Fig. 3c and 3d.

During the reaction between LTO and the LiPF6 electrolyte, an SEI layer composed of decomposition products (such as Li2CO3, ROCOOLi, and LiF) was formed on the surface of the LTO electrode along with the evolution of gaseous species, which play an important role in the electrochemical performance of the cell. During the reaction, the phase of the outer surface of the LTO also changed [24]. The thickness of the SEI layer depends on the duration of charge/discharge and the current density. At high current density, the SEI layer was insufficient to protect the outer surface of the LTO from decomposition. Hence, the number of reaction products, the thickness of the SEI layer, and the amount of gas produced increased. After coating N-GQDs on the LTO surface, this phenomenon was effectively reduced. TEM images of cycled LTO are shown in Fig. 4 a and 4b. In the cycled electrode of pristine LTO, the crystalline structure of the LTO surface was not clearly observed, due to the transformation of LTO (1 1 1) to LTO (2 2 2), (4 4 0), (4 4 4), and (5 3 3), anatase TiO2 (1 0 1), and a thicker layer of reaction products on the LTO surface; this was confirmed by FFT analysis at various points on the LTO surface (Fig. S11(a) of Supplementary Information). However, for the cycled N-GQD-encapsulated LTO, crystalline (1 1 1) LTO was clearly observed (Fig. 4c and 4d) by FFT and d-value analysis after cycling (Fig. S11(b) of Supplementary Information); this indicates that the N-GQDs protected the LTO surface from decomposition and a thinner SEI layer was formed.

A surface reaction occurred when the LTO surface was in contact with the electrolyte, resulting in a phase change of the outer LTO surface (Fig. 5 a) [24]. However, for LTO-NGQ20, the phase of the outermost surface of LTO was unchanged or only slightly changed owing to the protective N-GQDs layer (Fig. 5b). Accompanying the phase change of the LTO surface, H2, CO2, and CO gases were generated (Fig. 5c) [24]. In the LTO-NGQ20 electrode, gassing was reduced to a very low level (Fig. 5d). Thus, the LTO/electrolyte interfacial reactions could be suppressed by surface modification with N-GQDs and the resulting SEI film was smoother and thinner than that of uncoated LTO electrodes (similar to inorganic coatings, i.e., ZnO, AlF3, NiOx and TiNx) [30–33]. Both the LTO-NGQ and inorganic coatings themselves act as protecting the layer of the outermost surface of LTO. The initial charge–discharge profiles (@0.2C) of LTO and LTO-NGQ20 are shown in Fig. S12 of Supporting Information. The coulombic efficiency of LTO-NGQ20 is slightly lower (98.19%) than pure LTO (99.77%) [32].

Unfortunately, the LTO coated with inorganic materials electrodes showed a poor capacity (Table S3 of the Supporting Information) [30–33]. Both the formation and decomposition of SEI layer causes gassing [53]. In the case of carbon coated LTO, a thicker SEI layer is formed [24,28]. With increasing temperature, the SEI layer becomes unstable and started to decompose its carbonate species, which is accountable for gassing [53,54]. Therefore, due to the higher thickness of SEI layer in carbon coated LTO surface, carbon coated LTO is not safe at elevated temperature. Moreover, carbon materials have high reactivity with electrolyte solutions at elevated temperatures, which raises safety concerns [29]
Both XRD and FTIR analyses (Fig. S13(a) and S13(b) of Supplementary Information, respectively) revealed that a larger amount of Li2CO3 was formed in the pure LTO electrode than the LTO-NGQ20 one.

High-resolution XPS scans of the C 1 s and O 1 s peaks of the cycled LTO and LTO-NGQ20 electrodes are shown in Fig. 6 a and 6b, respectively. The peaks corresponding to C–C/CC and C–O of carbon (Super P) were absent in the cycled LTO electrode (due to the thicker SEI layer). These peaks were observed (with similar intensity) in both the as-prepared pristine LTO and LTO-NGQ20 electrodes (Fig. S14(a) of Supplementary Information). Four deconvoluted peaks of O 1 s at 530.91, 532.75, 533.55, and 534.81 eV corresponding to Li4Ti5O12, C-O-C/C-O-Li, ROCOOLi, and O–CO, respectively, were observed in the cycled LTO-NGQ20 electrode (Fig. 6b). However, the peak corresponding to Li4Ti5O12 was not observed in the cycled LTO electrode due to the thick SEI layer on the LTO surface, while it was observed (with similar intensity) in the as-prepared LTO and LTO-NGQ20 electrodes (Fig. S14(b) of Supplementary Information).

Fig. 6(c-f) show high-resolution XPS scans of Ti 2p, F 1 s, Li 1 s, and P 2p in as-prepared and cycled LTO and LTO-NGQ20 electrodes. The Ti 2p peaks of prepared electrodes shifted to higher binding energy than those of the powder samples, attributed to inelastic scattering of the interface between LTO (LTO-NGQ20) and the binder. Before cycling, the intensities of the Ti 2p, F 1 s, and Li 1 s peaks were similar in both the LTO and LTO-NGQ20 electrodes. Fig. 6c shows that the intensities of Ti 2p3/2 and Ti 2p1/2 peaks reduced after cycling for the LTO-NGQ20 electrode due to the thin SEI layer, while these peaks were absent in the cycled LTO electrode due to the thick SEI layer. In Fig. 6d, the F 1 s peak of PVDF was observed in both as-prepared LTO and LTO-NGQ20 electrodes. After cycling, a weak peak corresponding to PVDF and an intense one corresponding to LiF were obtained for the LTO-NGQ20 electrode; only an intense LiF peak was observed for the LTO electrode. In the as-prepared electrodes, the Li 1 s peak of Li2CO3 appeared, whereas additional peaks corresponding to LiPF6 and LiF appeared in the cycled electrodes. The cycled LTO electrode was etched for 1 min (∼2.52 nm) and 2 min (∼5.04 nm) using Ar sputtering to study the depth profile of the elements. Ti 2p peaks were not detected after etching for 1 min (Fig. S14(c) of Supplementary Information), indicating the deposition of an SEI layer of thickness of at least 7.52 nm (depth of etched surface ∼ 2.52 nm and ∼ 5 nm corresponding to the XPS probing depth at normal take-off angle) [55], while weak peaks were obtained after 2 min of etching. Similarly, the Li 1 s peak of LiF was observed after etching for 1 min, which disappeared after etching for 2 min and was replaced by the Li 1 s (Li2CO3) peak (Fig. S14(d) of Supplementary Information). Furthermore, N-GQDs protected the LTO from the interfacial reactions with the electrolyte, which resulted in no phase change of the outermost surface of LTO. Hence, the coating of N-GQDs on the LTO surface successfully reduced the SEI thickness, reducing the probability of gassing, and making the LIB more stable.

We demonstrated a novel LTO/N-GQDs/Super-P hierarchical anode material for LIB applications. This architecture enabled fast charge transfer and protected the LTO from degradation in air and surface reaction with the electrolyte. Highly N-doped GQDs induced a dense structure via charge interaction with LTO and Super P, which provided a shorter path for electron transfer. We demonstrated that the N-GQDs acted as an effective charge transfer layer to enhance the performance (∼23% at 50C) of LIBs and as a protective layer to prevent LTO degradation in air and/or surface reaction with the electrolyte. Moreover, a slight reduction in the Ti valence state of the outermost LTO surface, which enhanced the electronic conductivity and discharge capacity. The LTO-NGQ-based LIBs are durable and safe, even at high C-rates.

This work was financially supported by the Daegu Gyeongbuk Institute of Science & Technology (DGIST) R&D Program (Grant No. 18-NT-02) funded by the Ministry of Science, ICT and Future Planning and by the Energy Technology Development Program of the Korea Institute of Energy Technology Evaluation and Planning (KETEP, Grant No. 20163010012570), Republic of Korea. Author FK acknowledges the Center of Research Excellence in Renewable Energy (CoRERE), Research Institute, King Fahd University of Petroleum & Minerals (KFUPM), Kingdom of Saudi Arabia.

FK proposed the idea and design the experiments. MO performed device fabrication and characterization. FK and MO performed materials characterization. FK and JHK analyzed the results and co-wrote the paper.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cej.2019.03.161.
